section.title: What is xdock?
section.para: xdock is a software that displays small applets (also called docks) on the side of the screen. It seeks to emulate the WindowMaker dock applets.
The motivation for this project is to present a more modern platform to develop the dock applets. The WindowMaker docks, while great, have some shortcommings:

itemizedlist.listitem: it's hard to write applets, since it requires knowledge in Xlib, that most users don't have.
WindowMaker dock applets are too tightly integrated into WindowMaker itself, and don't work very well under other window managers, and much less under other operating systems.

section.para: xdock seeks to address these shortcommings by offering a dock server, where the docks themselves are clients that connect to this dock. Since the docks (the clients) and the server communicate using a standard protocol, xdock can easily be ported to other platfoms. The communication protocol is very simple, and applets can be built in any language since they respect the protocol. xdock also offers a C library, so that docks can be easily written in C.

section.title: Invoking xdock
section.para: xdock server can be easily started with the following command:

section.title: Server options
section.para: xdockserver accepts the following options:

listitem.para: Choose the color theme that will be used by xdockserver. The color theme define the default colors that'll be drawn on the applet. The default theme is led, where the applet will resemble a green LED panel. The alternative theme lcd will make the dock look like a LCD panel.
Choose the color of the background of the applet. This option can be used to match the color of the applet with the theme being used by the window manager. The color must be in the format #RRGGBB.
When the dock is dragged to the right border of the screen, it'll be automaticly attracted by n_pixels.

The IP addresses the server will listen to. The default value is localhost, which means the server will only accept clients that connect from the same machine the server is running. The alternative parameter is all, where the server will accept connections from any clients.
The IP port the server will listen to. The default value is 52530. If a different port is set, the same argument must be set on the clients.

When this option is set, the server will write debugging information about everything, including all raw communication with the clients. The information will be output to stdout. This is useful when writting a new client, to debug communication.

section.title: Config file
section.para: A config file can be created to store the options explained above. The config file will have the name ~/.xdock/config.rc and the options will be stored in the file in the format option = value. For options that don't have a value (such as debug), use true as the value.
A example file could be:

section.title: Client options
section.para: Each client will have its own arguments, that usually can be discovered with -h or --help. But they are required to present at least the following options:

listitem.para: The address where this client will connect to the xdock server. The default value is localhost.
The IP port where the client will connect to the server. The default value is 52530.

chapter.title: Developer documentation
chapter.para: This chapter explains how xdock work internally, and serves as a developer reference. For a more didactical approach, please refer to the tutorial.

section.title: Inner workings
section.para: This section explains how xdock works internally, and how clients can communicate with the server.

section.title: Communication between clients and server
section.para: Communication between server and client is always asynchronous (except for the initial handshake). Whenever the client sends a message to the server (for example, the PIXEL command), the server executes it immediately, but doesn't return anything to the client. In case of a error, the server will shut down the dock and close communications with the client.

The same happens when the server sends data to the client (for example, a mouse click event). It doesn't expect anything in return and, in the case of a error in the message (such as invalid syntax), the client can choose what it wants to do. The default behavior for clients using libxdock is for the client to display a error message and quit.
Here is the example of a communication between a simple client and the server:

programlistingco.programlisting: [Server > UnknownClient] XDOCK 1 [Server < UnknownClient] HELLO EXAMPLE [Server < EXAMPLE] PIXEL black 10 10 [Server < EXAMPLE] PIXEL black 11 10 [Server < EXAMPLE] PIXEL black 10 11 [Server > EXAMPLE] EVENT mouse_down 20 20 [Server > EXAMPLE] EVENT mouse_up 20 20 [Server < EXAMPLE] UPDATE

callout.para: The server sends a ACK to the client, informing the API version.

calloutlist.callout: The client responds the ACK, identifying itself.
The client draws three points on the dock memory.
The server tells the client about two mouse events.
The three point were drawn only in the memory. This command flushes the changes to the screen.

section.para: Please refer to the Communication Protocol Chapter to know more about what these commands mean.

section.title: Colors and themes
section.para: There are two types of colors in xdock: themed and non-themed.

Non-theme colors are colors that look always the same, and don't depend on the theme chosen by the user. This kind of color can be used for drawing a image, for example, where the colors won't change when the user switch themes. Non-themed colors are represented in the triplet format (#RRGGBB) or using X11 color names.
Themed colors are colors that change according to the theme. The colors are stored in the files $SHARE/themes.rc and ~/.xdock/themes.rc. The following colors are available:

itemizedlist.listitem: panel_bg: the background of the panel;
panel_lt and panel_sw: used for drawing the border of the panel;
unlit, halflit, lit and glow: user for drawing points on panel, in a increasing degree of brightenss;
warning_unlit, halfwarning and warning: used for drawing warning points on a panel.

section.para: Themed colors are used just like non-themed colors in the commands. For example, to draw a black point on the position 10x10, the command would be PANEL black 10 10. To draw using the "glow" color, the command would be PANEL glow 10 10. In this case, the pixel would be draw using the color set as glow for the theme the user chose.
Two themes are included in the distribution: led (default) and lcd. New themes can be created by editing the theme file.

section.title: Fonts
section.para: When writing text with the command WRITE, the user can choose one of the following builtin fonts:
The ability for a client to create new fonts is not yet available, but is planned for the future.
The fonts are always drawn using the colors unlit, halflit and lit set for the theme. Currently, there's no way to change the font colors. This feature is planned for the future.

section.title: XPM Images
section.para: Images are transfered from the clients to the servers by using the XPM format.

XPM is a image format created for the easy embedding of images in a C source file. It can be edited by hand (the editor VIM offers a useful syntax highlighting) of using a imaging software such as GIMP.
The Wikipedia page on XPM offers a good introduction to the format. A full documentation can be found here.
The images are sent to the client with the command SEND_XPM, and can be drawn with the command DRAW_IMAGE. For example, let's say that a client wants to send the following image to the server:
In this case, the command issued by the client would be the following:
Notice how the single dot ends the XPM listing. In this case, the client named the image as "blarg". To draw the image in the position 10x10, the client then sends the following command:

section.title: Themed XPM Images
section.para: Just like the basic commands, a image can also be drawn using themed colors. To do this, the second parameter of the color (which is usually c) must be replaced by s.
For example, let's say that a XPM color (blue) is:
To replace this color by glow (a themed color), this color would be replaced by:
To draw the image shown in the previous section using the colors panel_bg and lit, the image would sent to the server like this:

section.title: Events
section.para: Whenever a event occurs in the client, the server inform the client with the EVENT command. This is the only event that is sent from the server to the client (besides the handshake).
The syntax of the command is:

where type can be one of down and up, which represent a mouse button press and a mouse button release, respectively. The parameters x and y are the position (relative to the upper left border of the dock) where the event happened.
The client might choose to act upon a event, or to ignore it.

Currently, only mouse presses are available as events, and it is not possible to tell which button was pressed. Future versions will make the mouse button available, and will provide events for mouse move, dragging and keyboard.

section.title: Communication Protocol
section.para: This section documents a reference to all commands available in the communication protocol. xdock uses a textual approach on the protocol to facilitate development and debugging.

Most of the commands below have parameters. A parameter is always indicated by a lowercase parameters. Parameters named color follow the rules laid out in the color section. Parameters named x and y are positions in the dock, and can be any number between 0 and 95.

important.para: It's important to notice that all changes (such as drawing or writing text) are made only to memory. When all drawing is done, the command UPDATE must be called to flush the images to the dock.

table.title: List of xdock communication protocol commands

row.entry: This is the first command, which is sent from the server to the client when it first connects. The parameter api_version identifies the protocol version that the server will accept.

This is the response to the XDOCK command sent from the server. The server will shut down the connection if it receives any other reply. The name parameter identifies the client. Currently, the client name is only used when debugging with the option -d.
Draw a background panel using the theme colors.
Draw a single pixel on the dock.

entry.synopsis: LINE color x1 y2 x2 y2

row.entry: Draw a line in the dock.

entry.synopsis: RECTANGLE color x y w h

row.entry: Draw a hollow rectangle in the dock.

entry.synopsis: BOX color x y w h

row.entry: Draw a filled rectangle in the dock.

entry.synopsis: MOVEBOX x y w h move_x move_y bg_color

row.entry: This command moves a rectangular area defined by x, y, w and h. The area will be moved horizontally by move_x and vertically by move_y. These two parameters can be positive, zero or negative numbers. The move part of the box will be filled by the color defined by bg_color. This command is useful to draw moving graphs (see Drawing Graphics).

section.title: Writing xdock clients in languages other than C
